volumeTitle: Stichus. Trinummus. Truculentus. Tale of a Travelling Bag. Fragments
label: Trinummus (Section 4.2.917 - Section 4.2.973)

translation.label: Scaife English translation
translation.citation: Trinummus: The Three Pieces of Money

sections.label: Section 4.2.917
sections.original: Non est .
sections.translation: It isn ’ t that .

sections.label: Section 4.2.917b
sections.original: Callinicus ?
sections.translation: Callinicus ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.917c
sections.original: Non est .
sections.translation: No : it isn ’ t that .

sections.label: Section 4.2.917d
sections.original: Callimarchus ?
sections.translation: Or is it Callimachus ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.917e
sections.original: Nil agis .
sections.translation: ’ Tis in vain you suggest ; and , i ’ faith , I really don ’ t care one fillip about it , since I recollect enough myself for my own purpose .

sections.label: Section 4.2.919
sections.original: At enim multi Lesbonici sunt hic : nisi nomen patris
sections.translation: But there are many people here of the name of Lesbonicus ; unless you tell me the name of his father ,

sections.label: Section 4.2.920
sections.original: dices , non monstrare istos possum homines quos tu quaeritas .
sections.translation: I cannot show you these persons whom you are looking for . What is it like ? Perhaps we can find it out by guessing .

sections.label: Section 4.2.922
sections.original: Ad hoc exemplum est —
sections.translation: It is something like this : Char —

sections.label: Section 4.2.922b
sections.original: An Chares ? an Charmides ?
sections.translation: Chares ? Or Charicles ? Or is it Charmides ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.922c
sections.original: Enim Charmides .
sections.translation: Ah ! that ’ s he ; may the Deities confound him .

sections.label: Section 4.2.924
sections.original: Te potius bene dicere aequomst homini amico , quam male .
sections.translation: I have said to you once before already ⋯ that it is proper for you rather to speak well of a man that is your friend , than to curse him .

sections.label: Section 4.2.925
sections.original: Satin inter labra atque dentes latuit vir minimi preti ?
sections.translation: Isn ’ t it the fact [*] that this most worthless fellow has lain perdu between my lips and my teeth ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.926
sections.original: Ne male loquere apsenti amico .
sections.translation: Don ’ t you be cursing an absent friend .

sections.label: Section 4.2.926b
sections.original: Quíd ergo ille ignavissumus
sections.translation: Why , then , did this most rascally fellow hide himself away from me ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.927b
sections.original: Si appellasses , respondisset , nomine .
sections.translation: If you had only called him , he would have answered to his name . But where is he himself now ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.928b
sections.original: Pol illúm reliqui ad Rhadamantem in Cecropia insula .†
sections.translation: Troth , I left him at Rhadama [*] , in the isle of Apeland .

sections.label: Section 4.2.929
sections.original: Quis homo est me insipientior , qui ipse , egomet ubi sim , quaeritem ?
sections.translation: ( aside .) What person is there a greater simpleton than I , who myself am making inquiries where I am ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.930
sections.original: sed nil disconducit huic rei . quid ais ? quid hoc quod te rogo ?
sections.translation: But it is by no means unimportant to this present purpose . What do you say as —?

sections.label: Section 4.2.931
sections.original: quos locos adiisti ?
sections.translation: I ask you this . [*] What places have you visited ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.931b
sections.original: Nimium mirimodis mirabiles .
sections.translation: Places exceedingly wonderful in astonishing ways .

sections.label: Section 4.2.932
sections.original: Lubet audire , nisi molestumst .
sections.translation: I should like to hear about them , unless it is inconvenient .

sections.label: Section 4.2.932b
sections.original: Quin discupio dicere .
sections.translation: Really I quite long to tell you . First of all we were conveyed to Pontus , to the land of Arabia [*] .

sections.label: Section 4.2.934
sections.original: Eho an etiam Arabiast in Ponto ?
sections.translation: How now ; is Arabia then in Pontus ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.934b
sections.original: Est : non illa ubi tus gignitur ,
sections.translation: It is . Not that Arabia where frankincense is produced ,

sections.label: Section 4.2.935
sections.original: sed ubi apsinthium fit atque cunila gallinacea .
sections.translation: but where the wormwood grows [*] , and the wild marjoram which the poultry love .

sections.label: Section 4.2.936
sections.original: Nimium graphicum hunc nugatorem . sed ego sum insipientior ,
sections.translation: An extremely ingenious knave this . But the greater simpleton I , to be asking of this fellow from what place I have come back , a thing which I know , and he does not know ; except that I have a mind to try how he will get out of it at last . But what say you further ? Whither did you go next from thence ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.939b
sections.original: Si animum advortes , eloquar .
sections.translation: If you give me your attention , I will tell you .

sections.label: Section 4.2.940
sections.original: ad caput amnis , quod de caelo exoritur sub solio Iovis .
sections.translation: To the source of the river which arises out of the heavens , from beneath the throne of Jupiter .

sections.label: Section 4.2.941
sections.original: Sub solio Iovis ?
sections.translation: Beneath the throne of Jupiter ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.941b
sections.original: Ita dico .
sections.translation: Yes : I say so .

sections.label: Section 4.2.941c
sections.original: E caelo ?
sections.translation: Out of the heavens ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.941d
sections.original: Atque e medio quidem .
sections.translation: Aye , out of the very middle .

sections.label: Section 4.2.942
sections.original: Eho an etiam ín caelum escendisti ?
sections.translation: How now ; and did you ascend even to the heavens ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.942b
sections.original: Immo horiola advecti sumus ,
sections.translation: Yes : we were carried in a little skiff [*] right on , up the river , against the tide .

sections.label: Section 4.2.943b
sections.original: Eho an tu etiam vidisti Iovem ?
sections.translation: And did you see Jupiter as well ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.944
sections.original: Alii di isse ad villam aiebant servis depromptum cibum .
sections.translation: The other Gods said that he had gone to his country-house , to dole out the victuals for his slaves .

sections.label: Section 4.2.945
sections.original: deinde porro —
sections.translation: Then , after that —

sections.label: Section 4.2.945b
sections.original: Deinde porro nolo quicquam praedices .
sections.translation: Then after that — I don ’ t want you to relate anything more .

sections.label: Section 4.2.946b
sections.original: Abe o hercle , si es molestus . nam pudicum neminem ,
sections.translation: Troth , I ’ m silent , if it ’ s troublesome .

sections.label: Section 4.2.947
sections.original: Pax , refe rre oportet , qui aps terra ad caelum pervenerit .
sections.translation: Why , no decent person [*] ought to tell it , who has gone from the earth to heaven .

sections.label: Section 4.2.948
sections.original: Facia m ita ut te velle video . sed monstra hosce homines mihi ,
sections.translation: I ’ ll leave you , as I see you wish it . But point me out these persons whom I am looking for , and to whom I must deliver these letters .

sections.label: Section 4.2.950
sections.original: Quid ais ? tu nunc si forte eumpse Charmidem conspexeris ,
sections.translation: What say you ? If now perchance you were to see Charmides himself , him , I mean , who you say gave you these letters , would you know the man ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.952b
sections.original: Ne tu me edepol arbitrare beluam ,
sections.translation: By my troth now , do you take me to be a brute beast , who really am not able to recognise the person with whom I have been spending my life ? And would he have been such a fool as to entrust to me a thousand

sections.label: Section 4.2.955
sections.original: Philippum , quod me aurum deferre iussit ad gnatum suom
sections.translation: Philippean pieces , which gold he bade me carry to his son , and to his friend Callicles , to whom he said that he had entrusted his affairs ? Would he have entrusted them to me if he had not known me , and I him , very intimately ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.958
sections.original: Enim vero ego nunc sycophantae huic sycophantari volo ,
sections.translation: I really have a longing now to swindle this swindler , if I can cozen him out of these thousand Philippean pieces

sections.label: Section 4.2.960
sections.original: quod sibi me dedisse dixit , quem ego qui sit homo nescio
sections.translation: which he has said that I have given to him . A person , that I know not who he is , and have never beheld him with my eyes before this day , should I be entrusting gold to him ? A man , to whom , if his life were at stake , I would not entrust a dump of lead . This fellow must be adroitly dealt with by me . Hallo ! Mister Hush , I want three words with you .

sections.label: Section 4.2.964
sections.original: Vel trecentis .
sections.translation: Even three hundred , if you like .

sections.label: Section 4.2.964b
sections.original: Haben tu id aurum quod accepisti a Charmide ?
sections.translation: Have you that gold which you received from Charmides ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.965
sections.original: Atque etiam Philippúm , numeratum illius in mensa manu ,
sections.translation: Yes , and Philippeans , too , counted out on the table with his own hand , a thousand pieces .

sections.label: Section 4.2.966b
sections.original: Nempe ab ipso id accepisti Charmide ?
sections.translation: You received it , you mean , from Charmides himself ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.967
sections.original: Mirum quin ab avo eius aut próavo acciperem , qui sunt mortui .
sections.translation: ’ Twere a wonder if I had received it of his father , or of his grandfather , who are dead .

sections.label: Section 4.2.968
sections.original: Adulescens , cedo dum istuc aurum mi .
sections.translation: Then , young man , hand me over this gold .

sections.label: Section 4.2.968b
sections.original: Quod ego aurum dem tibi ?
sections.translation: What gold am I to give you ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.969
sections.original: Quod a me te accepisse fassu'sfassus es .
sections.translation: That which you have owned you received from me .

sections.label: Section 4.2.969b
sections.original: Aps te accepisse ?
sections.translation: Received from you ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.969c
sections.original: Ita loquor .
sections.translation: Yes , I say so .

sections.label: Section 4.2.970
sections.original: Quis tu homo es ?
sections.translation: Who are you ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.970b
sections.original: Qui mille nummum tibi dedi ego sum Charmides .
sections.translation: I am Charmides , who gave you the thousand pieces of money .

sections.label: Section 4.2.971
sections.original: Neque edepol tu is es neque hodie is umquam eris , auro huic quidem .
sections.translation: I ’ faith , you are not he ; and this day , you never shall be he , for this gold , at any rate . Away with you , if you please , you impostor ! ( Aside .) You are trying to cheat the cheater .

sections.label: Section 4.2.973
sections.original: Charmides ego sum .
sections.translation: I am Charmides .
